comment.body: Kai volatility fund has terrible results. Larry Fink was trolling him on it on X. Cem knows his Greeks and vol stuff but he makes wrong calls none the less. comment.body: Weekly post is a little contradictory. "Plus the bad (potentially deflationary datas)" .. but it is exactly this data print that would allow QE to resume (net-positive). Similarly, a ton of conjecture unilaterally stating what the "ultimate goal" is behind tariffs. Similarly "Holy Aliance 2.0" .. all rooted in personal bias, none in objective data reading. However enjoy the work as ever, and notes re: flows are interesting. Conversation is good. Thanks for producing.

comment.body:
Nuances aside, can someone confirm my understanding of the big picture based on my readings :

- China is the final boss. 
- US wants holy alliance 2.0 to weaken China. 
- To achieve holy alliance, US needs Russia. 
- To get Russia, there needs to be ceasefire. 
- To get to ceasefire, US implements tariff to bring everyone to nego table.
- Tariff has 2 tracks - pressure EU and China.
- EU so it agrees to ceasefire, China so it abandons EU.

Right now we're sort of at a crossroad where we need to land on a deal somewhere

comment.body: If the United States wants China to return to the negotiating table, relying on tariff pressure is no longer feasible. China will demand the concessions it seeks, such as the normalization of trade relations. In recent years, China has also been developing trade with other countries, gradually reducing the impact of U.S. economic pressure

comment.body: The world is currently in an unavoidable economic downturn cycle, which will inevitably lead to a rise in protectionism. During this period, countries are resorting to aggressive measures to externalize their internal conflicts. The question is: Can technology help break free from such intense confrontations and enable a more refined approach to navigating the downturn? In my view, much of humanity's chaos is self-inflicted—can technology offer a solution?
comment.raw_body: The world is currently in an unavoidable economic downturn cycle, which will inevitably lead to a rise in protectionism. The question is: Can technology help break free from such intense confrontations and enable a more refined approach to navigating the downturn? In my view, much of humanity's chaos is self-inflicted—can technology offer a solution?

comment.body:
I think it’s a chicken and egg 
Protectionism leads to the downturn 
And the protectionism is a by product of or maybe the result of populism 
So the next layer is what causes populism to rise ?
A sense of unfairness for one but on a political level it’s a break down of pluralism
